Malaysia, Singapore Finalizing 'Master Plan' for Investment Hub as Billions Pour In
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong


KUALA LUMPUR, Malaysia--Malaysia and Singapore are finalizing the "master plan" for a special economic zone that has already netted over $17 billion in investment.

The Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone is proving to be a gold mine so far, drawing in global firms such as Kuehne+Nagel and ResMed with incentives like tax breaks.

It secured $17.31 billion in approved investments in the first nine months of 2025, emerging as a key beneficiary of multinational companies' efforts to diversify amid rising geopolitical tensions.

The zone was formed in January last year to develop high-value industries, letting investors leverage Johor's land and cost advantages alongside Singapore's strong financial services and infrastructure.

Since then, officials have been moving forward with measures to turn the area into a regional tech and business hub.

A key step in the push is finalizing the so-called investment blueprint and masterplan, which Malaysian officials said Friday will be launched in March.

The plan will outline priority sectors and an implementation roadmap, serving as a reference for investors and guiding strategic capital into the zone.

The update came after Malaysia's economy minister, Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir, met Singapore's minister for national development, Chee Hong Tat, to reaffirm their commitment to the partnership.


Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Roche Holding AG Reports Earnings Results for the Full Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
Roche Holding AG reported earnings results for the full year ended December 31, 2025. For the full year, the company reported sales was CHF 61,516 million compared to CHF 60,495 million a year ago. Revenue was CHF 63,356 million compared to CHF 62,395 million a year ago.

Net income was CHF 12,880 million compared to CHF 8,277 million a year ago.

01-30-26 0016ET

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing : Trump Expected to Pick Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

EU GDP; Germany GDP, unemployment, CPI; France GDP, consumer spending; U.K. money and credit; trading updates from CaixaBank, Electrolux, Signify, SKF, Autoliv, Airtel Africa

Opening Call:

European stock futures advanced early Friday. Asian stock indexes largely fell; the dollar rose; Treasury yields edged higher; while oil and gold futures fell.

Equities:

Stock futures point to a higher open in European markets, with traders focused on President Trump's pick for the next Federal Reserve chair, which he said he plans to announce Friday.

Trump's advisers have been told that he is expected to nominate Kevin Warsh, a central bank insider-turned-critic, as Fed chair, according to people familiar with the matter.

Investors widely expect any of the four candidates interviewed to move quickly to implement Trump's demands for lower rates. Futures markets indicate investors are betting, on balance, that the Fed will keep interest rates steady until a new chair takes over in May.

Forex:

The greenback strengthened amid prospects of the U.S. government avoiding a shutdown, which could bolster the appeal of U.S. assets. Trump and Senate Democrats said there is a deal to avert a partial government shutdown and temporarily fund the Department of Homeland Security.

Also, U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessent's recent reaffirmation of strong USD policy has helped to support the greenback, OCBC said. However, USD weakness could extend if investors doubt that "maximum U.S. policy uncertainty" has passed, OCBC added.

Bonds:

Fixed-income investors should look into non-U.S. government debt for higher return, Wellington Management's Brij Khurana said. Long-term Treasury yields have been elevated due in part to worries that Washington will have to issue too much debt to finance a gaping budget deficit.

Khurana said some countries with sound credit ratings don't face the same risk and are likely to enter an interest-rate raising cycle while the Fed is easing. He also sees value in inflation-protected bonds and is bracing for a weakening in corporate bonds as companies borrow to finance AI capex.

Energy:

Oil futures fell early Friday, pulling back after recent gains.

Focus in oil markets remains on Iran. Trump has been increasing pressure on Iran, while Iran has issued a warning that it plans to run a drill next week that would include live firing in the Strait of Hormuz, Westpac said. "However, we stick to the view that regime change in Iran is likely to be chaotic and certainly nothing like the [U.S.] clinical extraction of Maduro or the surgical strikes on Fordow," Westpac said.

Metals:

Gold prices just raced across the $5,000 mark. Now J.P. Morgan thinks they can zoom past $8,000 in the years ahead. One reason is retail investors are relying more on gold instead of fixed income as a hedge against U.S. stock market declines.

Panigirtzoglou said gold has already surged from about 1% of private investors' overall portfolios a decade ago to 3% today. He projected the total will rise to 4.6% in the future. That extra demand, he suggested, would imply a "theoretical price" of $8,000 to $8,500.

-

The London Metal Exchange delayed the start of trading on Friday due to technical issues, with trading offline for roughly one hour.

Copper prices swung sharply overnight, up around 40% since crashing at the end of July.

"You've got your typical supply-demand dynamics from economics working in real-time," said Clarksons Securities analyst Roald Ross. "We see fundamentals pointing to a strong case for high copper prices in the years to come," Ross said.

-

Iron ore rose slightly in Asia, reversing earlier losses in a volatile start to the day. As China's steel mills enter the off-peak season, Nanhua Futures expects to see weakness in both supply and demand for the steelmaking material.

However, Nanhua thinks downside for iron ore is limited given that steel market dynamics remain relatively supportive and the overall industrial chain remains profitable.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Trump Expected to Pick Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair

WASHINGTON-President Trump's advisers have been told that he is expected to nominate Kevin Warsh, a central bank insider-turned-critic, as Federal Reserve chair, according to people familiar with the matter.

Trump's selection of Warsh would bring to an end a months-long internal deliberation among the president and his top advisers over who should lead the central bank.

Trump, Democrats Say Deal Reached to Avert Shutdown

WASHINGTON-President Trump and Senate Democrats said there is a deal to avert a partial government shutdown and temporarily fund the Department of Homeland Security, as negotiations continue on proposed restrictions to immigration enforcement.

Under the agreement, the Senate would move quickly to pass five of the six spending bills that already have cleared the House-funding most of the federal government for the rest of the fiscal year ending Sept. 30-while passing a two-week extension for DHS.

London Metal Exchange Resumes Trade After Technical Glitch

The London Metal Exchange delayed the start of trading on Friday due to technical issues, temporarily stifling activity as commodities markets experience one of their wildest weeks.

Trading on the LME, which sets benchmark prices for a range of commodities including copper and aluminum, was offline for roughly one hour. The LME's electronic platform usually starts trading at 1.00 a.m. London time.

Adidas to Buy Back EUR1 Billion in Stock After Preliminary Fourth-Quarter Revenue Shows Increase

Adidas AG is introducing a share-repurchase program for 2026 after it reported higher preliminary revenue for the most recent quarter.

The German footwear and apparel company on Thursday said its preliminary fourth-quarter financial results showed revenue of 6.08 billion euros ($7.27 billion), rising from EUR5.97 billion a year ago.

China Agrees to Lower Tariffs by Half on U.K. Whisky

China has agreed to cut its tariffs in half on whisky from the U.K. during Prime Minister Keir Starmer's visit to Beijing, the U.K. government said Thursday.

"Our whisky distilleries are the jewel in Scotland's crown," Starmer said. The government said it expects whisky exports to China to be worth roughly 250 million pounds ($345.2 million) to the U.K. economy over the next five years as tariffs are lowered to 5% from 10%.

OpenAI Plans Fourth-Quarter IPO in Race to Beat Anthropic to Market

OpenAI is laying the groundwork for a public listing in the fourth quarter of this year, people familiar with the matter said, accelerating its plans as competition with rival Anthropic intensifies.

The $500 billion startup is holding informal talks with Wall Street banks about a potential initial public offering, people familiar with the matter said, and is growing its finance team. That includes the hire of a new chief accounting officer, Ajmere Dale, and a new corporate business finance officer, Cynthia Gaylor, who will oversee investor relations.

Amazon in Talks to Invest Up to $50 Billion in OpenAI

Amazon.com is in talks to invest up to $50 billion in OpenAI, according to people familiar with the matter, in what would be a giant bet on the hot AI startup.

The ChatGPT maker is seeking up to $100 billion in new capital from investors, a round that could value it at as much as $830 billion, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.

Apple Posts Blowout iPhone Sales, but Investors Focus on Higher Costs

Apple reported blowout iPhone sales and profits in the December quarter, but shares stayed flat after-hours because of investor concerns about rising costs.

Revenue from iPhones rose 23% to $85.3 billion compared with the prior year as customers, excited by the new iPhone 17 lineup, upgraded their devices faster than usual. That powered Apple to record quarterly sales of nearly $144 billion, results that exceeded Wall Street expectations.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: CK Hutchison Shares Fall After Panama Court Strikes Down Port Contract
data.description:
By Tracy Qu


CK Hutchison's shares fell after Panama's Supreme Court ruled against the Hong Kong conglomerate running two ports on the Panama Canal.

The share decline came after the court said in a brief statement that license terms granted to CK Hutchison to run the ports of Balboa on the Pacific Coast and Cristobal on the Atlantic side were unconstitutional.

CK Hutchinson's shares fell 1.3% in early trade Friday and extended losses to 4.4% at the midday break following the court decision. The benchmark Hang Seng Index was down 1.8% as of the lunch break.

CK Hutchison didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

The decision had been closely watched in Washington and Beijing, and is seen as a key step in their broader dispute over the Panama Canal. At least 5% of global trade flows through the Panama Canal.


Write to Tracy Qu at tracy.qu@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-30-26 0014ET

Indonesia Stock Exchange CEO Quits in Wake of Market Rout
data.description:
By Amanda Lee


The Indonesia Stock Exchange's chief executive has resigned after a two-day selloff sparked by concerns about the investability of local securities.

Iman Rachman told reporters Friday that he is stepping down to take responsibility for the recent volatility.

Indonesia stocks rebounded in morning trade, continuing a roller-coaster ride started after index provider MSCI Inc. warned of a possible downgrade of the Southeast Asian market's status.

That sent stocks tumbling Wednesday, and the Jakarta Composite Index to its lowest levels since November. An 8% drop Thursday triggered a trading halt, before the index clawed back ground to finish 1.1% lower.

On Friday, it bounced back, rising 1.2%.

Rachman said he hopes that the market will keep progressing after his resignation.

The market chaos ensued after MSCI froze index-related changes for Indonesian stocks over what it said were significant investor concerns about company ownership structures. That added to simmering anxiety about fiscal overreach and economic headwinds.

Regulators have vowed to address the issues raised by MSCI.

The efforts will likely boost clarity and transparency in securities trading, DBS's Radhika Rao said, noting plans including a potential doubling in minimum free-float requirements for listed companies.

"We expect more affirmative steps to assuage concerns of the investor community and global index providers, while ironing out opacity of business structure and any remnant governance issues in the medium-term," Rao said.


Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-30-26 0013ET

China's factory activity expected to stall at start of new year
data.description:
BEIJING, Jan 30 (Reuters) - China's factory activity likely stalled in the first month of 2026, a Reuters poll showed on Friday, as still sluggish domestic demand held back production.

The survey of 25 economists forecast the official purchasing managers' index (PMI) would dip to 50.0 in January from 50.1 in December. The data is due on Saturday.

The projection follows a surprise recovery in December, when China's vast manufacturing sector snapped a record eight-month contraction streak.

The headline manufacturing PMI likely benefited from this year's unusually late Lunar New Year holidays, analysts at Mizuho Securities said.

"With the holiday landing in late February, many migrant workers may not return to their jobs until mid-March, prompting factories to front-load their manufacturing activity to the beginning of the year," Mizuho said.

Factory activity could face more pressure next month when holiday travel gets into full swing. The Lunar New Year holiday started in late January last year.

China's economy grew 5% in 2025, hitting Beijing's official target, with exports playing a heavy-lifter despite U.S. tariff pressures. Growth, however, has remained uneven. Retail sales weakened further in the final quarter, dragging fourth-quarter GDP growth to a three-year low.

Policymakers have vowed to make boosting domestic demand their top priority this year. The government front-loaded 62.5 billion yuan ($8.99 billion) from ultra-long special treasury bond funds to support its scheme offering consumers subsidies to replace a range of products from home appliances to smartphones.

But persistent weakness in the property market, a major source of household wealth, would mean authorities have their work cut out to persuade consumers to open up their wallets. 

Subdued demand at home has also pushed manufacturers to slash prices to defend market share, intensifying deflationary pressures and weighing on corporate earnings.

Beijing has stepped in to rein in excessive price wars under its so-called "anti-involution" crackdown. The crackdown helped bolster the bottom line for companies.

Industrial profit rose last year for the first time since 2021, and grew 5.3% in December from a year earlier, reversing a 13.1% decline in November.

China is likely to set this year's official growth target between 4.5% and 5%, the South China Morning Post reported, as policymakers take a cautious approach to stimulus with a stock market bubble on their minds.

Analysts polled by Reuters forecast the private sector RatingDog PMI to stand at 50.3, up from 50.1 a month prior. The data will be released on February 2. 

($1 = 6.9489 Chinese yuan)

(Reporting by Ethan Wang and Ryan Woo; Polling by Pranoy Krishna in Bengaluru and Jing Wang in Shanghai; Editing by Sam Holmes)

Low risk of Nipah virus spread beyond India, says WHO
data.description:
HYDERABAD, Jan 30 (Reuters) - The risk of the deadly Nipah virus spreading from India is low, the World Health Organisation said on Friday, adding that it does not recommend any travel or trade restrictions after the South Asian nation reported two cases of the virus infection.

(Reporting by Rishika Sadam; Editing by Tom Hogue)

By Rishika Sadam

data.title: Japan carefully considering implications of consumption tax suspension, finance minister says
data.description:
TOKYO, Jan 30 (Reuters) - Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is carefully considering the implications for fiscal sustainability of a consumption tax suspension and is consulting with the Finance Ministry, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Friday.

Takaichi has proposed halting the 8% consumption tax on food and drinks for a period of two years, which precipitated a jump in government bond yields and a decline in the yen on market fears it could require funding through more debt issuance.

Speaking at a campaign event in support of a lower house election candidate in Tokyo, Katayama said that she had explained the justification for the tax cut at the World Economic Forum's annual gathering in Davos earlier this month and that current market conditions had stabilised.

(Reporting by Tamiyuki Kihara; Writing by Anton Bridge; Editing by Jamie Freed)
